2.1 Introduction to System of Linear Equations

  • Definitions
    • A linear equation in the nn n﻿ variables x1,x2,...,xnx_1, x_2, ..., x_n x1​,x2​,...,xn​﻿ is defined to be one that can be expressed in the form

    where a1,a2,...,ana_1, a_2,...,a_n a1​,a2​,...,an​﻿ and bbb﻿ are constants and the aia_iai​﻿'s are not all zero.

    • A homogeneous linear equation in the nn n﻿ variables x1,x2,...,xnx_1, x_2, ..., x_n x1​,x2​,...,xn​﻿ is defined to be one that can be expressed in the form

    where a1,a2,...,ana_1, a_2,...,a_n a1​,a2​,...,an​﻿ are constants and the aia_iai​﻿'s are not all zero.

    • A solution of a linear equation is defined to be a squence of nnn﻿ numbers s1,s2,...,sns_1,s_2, ..., s_ns1​,s2​,...,sn​﻿ such that the equation is satisfied when we subtitute
    • A finite set of linear equations in the nnn﻿ variables x1,x2,...,xnx_1, x_2, ..., x_n x1​,x2​,...,xn​﻿ is called a system of linear equations or a linear system. The variables in a linear system are called the unknowns.
    • A finite set of homogeneous linear equations in the nnn﻿ variables x1,x2,...,xnx_1, x_2, ..., x_n x1​,x2​,...,xn​﻿ is called a homogeneous system of linear equations or a homogeneous linear system.
    • A solution of a system of linear equations in the nnn﻿ variables x1,x2,...,xnx_1, x_2, ..., x_n x1​,x2​,...,xn​﻿ is defined to be a sequence of nn n﻿ numbers s1,s2,...,sns_1,s_2, ..., s_ns1​,s2​,...,sn​﻿ such that

    is a solution of every linear equation in the system.

    • A linear system having the same number nn n﻿ of equtions as unknowns is called a square system.
    • A system of linear equations that has no solutions is said to be inconsistent. A system of linear equations that has at least on solution is said to be consistent.
    • A system of mmm﻿ linear equations in nn n﻿ unknows (or nnn﻿ variables) will be written

    where x1,x2,...,xnx_1, x_2, ..., x_n x1​,x2​,...,xn​﻿ are the unknowns and aija_{ij} aij​﻿'s and bib_ibi​﻿'s are constants.

    • A linear system can be abbreviated by writing only the rectangular array of numbers:

    which is called the augmented matrix for the system. The matrix

    which has been augmented by the column vector of constants

    is called the matrix of coefficients or the coefficient matrix of the linear system.

    • Matrices that can be obtained from one another by a finite sequence of elementary row operations are said to be row equivalent.
  • Theorems
    • Every system of linear equations has zero, one, or infinitely many solutions; there are no other possibilities.
  • Elementary Row Operations
    1. [Row scaling] Multiplying a row through by a nonzero constant.
    1. [Row interchange] Interchange two rows. That is, interchange the iii﻿th and jjj﻿th row vectors of a matrix where i≠ji\neq ji​=j﻿.
    1. [Row addition] Add a multiple of one row to another. That is, add to the iii﻿th row vector of a matrix sss﻿ times the jjj﻿th row vector where i≠ji\neq ji​=j﻿.

2.2 Solving Linear Systems by Row Reduction

  • Definitions
    • A matrix is said to be in row echelon form if it has the following properties:
      1. If a row does not constist entirely of zeros, then the first nonzero number in the row is 1 which is called a leading 1 or the pivot for that row.
      1. If there are any rows that consist entirely of zeros, then they are grouped together at the bottom of the matrix.
      1. In any two successive rows that do not consist entirely of zeros, the leading 1 in the lower row occurs farther to the right than the leading 1 in the higher row.
      1. Each column that contains a leading 1 has zeros everywhere else.
    • A matrix in row echelon form is said to be in reduced row echelon form if each column that contains a leading 1 has zeros everywhere else.
    • For a linear system Ax=bAx=b Ax=b﻿, the unknowns corresponding to the column positions where the leading 1's do not occurin the row echelon forms of the coefficient matrix AAA﻿ are called the free variables.
    • The column vectors of a matrix AAA﻿ that lie in the column positions where the leading 1's occur in the row echelon forms of AAA﻿ are called the pivot columns of AAA﻿.n nj pl r09i0
    • A submatrix (or block) of a matrix AA A﻿ is defined to be an array obrtained by deleting any combination of rows and columns from AA A﻿.
    • The column vectors of a matrix AAA﻿ that lie in the column positions where the leading 1's occur in the row echelon forms of AAA﻿ are called the pivot columns of AAA﻿.
    • The zero vector in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn﻿ is called the trivial solution of a homogeneous linear system with nnn﻿ unknowns. All other solutions, if any, are called nontrivial solutions.
  • Theorems
    • A homogeneous linear system has only the trivial solution or it has infinitely many solutions; there are no other possibilities.
      • proof

        Assume that a homogeneous linear system with nnn﻿ unknowns has some nontrivial solution, say x=(x1,x2,...,xn)x=(x_1,x_2,...,x_n)x=(x1​,x2​,...,xn​)﻿. Then kxkxkx﻿ is a solution for any scalar kkk﻿. Thus the system has infinitely many solution ■\blacksquare■﻿

    • (The Dimension Theorem for Homogeneous Linear Systems). If a homogeneous linear system has nnn﻿ unknowns and the reduced row echelon form of its aurmented matrix has rrr﻿ nonzero rows, then the system has n−rn-rn−r﻿ free variables.
    • A homogeneous linear system with more unknowns than equations has infinitely many solutions
      • proof

        If a homogeneous linear system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ has mmm﻿ equations and nnn﻿ unknowns with m<nm<nm<n﻿, then the number of nonzero rows of the reduced row echelon form of AAA﻿ is less than nnn﻿. Thus there is at least one free variables ■\blacksquare■﻿

  • Gauss Elimination
    • Step 1.

    Locate the leftmost column that does not consist entirely of zeros. That is, if the first column contains only zero entries, then cross it off mentally. Continue in this fashion until the first column of the remaining matrix has a nonzero entry or unril the columns are exhausted.

    • Step 2.

    Interchange the top row with another row, if necessary, to bring a nonzero entry to the top of the column found in Step 1. That is, use row interchange, if necessary, to obtain a nonzero entry in the top row of the first column of the remaining matrix.

    • Step 3.

    If the entry that is now at the top of the column found in Step 1 is aaa﻿, then multiply the first row by 1/a1/a1/a﻿ in order to introduce a leading 1.

    • Step 4.

    Add suitable multiples of the top row to the rows below so that all entries below leading 1 become zero. That is, for each row below that has a nonzero entry rrr﻿ in the first column, add −r-r−r﻿ times the top row to that row to create a zero in the first column. In this fashion, create zeros below 1 in the entire first column of the remaining matrix.

    • Step 5.

    Cover the top row in the matrix and begin again with Step 1 applied to the submatrix that remains. That is, mentally cross off this first column and the first row of the matrix to obtain a smaller matrix. Go back to Step 1 and repeat the process with this smaller matrix until either no rows or no columns remain.

  • Gauss-Jordan Elimination
    • Step 1.

    Follow Step 1 to Step 5 in Gauss Elimination

    • Step 2.

    Beginning with the last nonzero row and working upward, add suitable multiples of each row to the rows above to introduce zeros above the leading 1's.

3.1 Operations on Matrices

  • Definitions
    • A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers. The numbers in the array are called the entries in the matrix. The size of a matrix is described by specifying the number of rows (horizontal lines) and columns (vertical lines) that occur in the matrix.
    • A matrix A=(aij)A=(a_{ij})A=(aij​)﻿ with nnn﻿ row and nn n﻿ columns is called a square matrix of order nnn﻿. The entries a11,a12,...,anna_{11},a_{12},..., a_{nn}a11​,a12​,...,ann​﻿ are said to form the main diagnal of AAA﻿.
    • Two matrices are defined to be equal if they have the same size and their corresponding entries are equal.
    • Let AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ be matrices withthe same size. The sum A+BA+BA+B﻿ is defined to be the matrix obtained by adding the entries of BBB﻿ to the corresponding entries of AAA﻿. That is, the entry in the iii﻿th row and jj j﻿th column of A+BA+B A+B﻿ is aij+bija_{ij}+b_{ij}aij​+bij​﻿ where aija_{ij}aij​﻿ and bijb_{ij}bij​﻿ are the ijijij﻿th entries of AAA﻿ and BB B﻿ respectively.
    • Let AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ be matrices withthe same size. The difference A−BA-BA−B﻿ is defined to be the matrix obtained by subtracting the entries of BBB﻿ from the corresponding entries of AAA﻿. That is, the entry in the iii﻿th row and jj j﻿th column of A−BA-B A−B﻿ is aij−bija_{ij}-b_{ij}aij​−bij​﻿ where aija_{ij}aij​﻿ and bijb_{ij}bij​﻿ are the ijijij﻿th entries of AAA﻿ and BB B﻿ respectively.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a matrix and kkk﻿ be a scalar. The product kAkAkA﻿ is defined to be the matrix obtained by multiplying each entry of AAA﻿ by kkk﻿. That is, the entry in the iii﻿th row and jjj﻿th column of kAkAkA﻿ is kaijka_{ij}kaij​﻿ where aija_{ij}aij​﻿ is the ijijij﻿th entry of AAA﻿.
    • A matrix consisting of a single row is called a row vector. A matrix consisting of a single comlumn is called a column vector.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix and xxx﻿ be an n×1n\times 1n×1﻿ column vector. The product AxAxAx﻿ is defined to be the m×1m\times 1m×1﻿ column vector that results by forming the linear combination of the column vectors of AA A﻿ that has the entries of xxx﻿ as coefficients. More precisely, if the column vectors of AAA﻿ are a1,a2,...,ana_1, a_2,..., a_na1​,a2​,...,an​﻿ and x=(x1,x2,...,xn)x = (x_1, x_2, ..., x_n)x=(x1​,x2​,...,xn​)﻿, then
    • Let AA A﻿ be an m×sm\times sm×s﻿ matrix and BB B﻿ be an s×ns\times ns×n﻿ matrix whose column vectors are b1,b2,...,bnb_1, b_2, ...,b_nb1​,b2​,...,bn​﻿. The product ABABAB﻿ is defined to be the m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix given by
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. The transpose of AAA﻿, denoted by ATA^\mathsf{T} AT﻿, is defined to be the n×mn\times mn×m﻿ matrix that is obtained by making the rows of AAA﻿ into columns.
    • Let AA A﻿ be a square matrix. The trace of AAA﻿, denoted by tr(A)tr(A)tr(A)﻿, is defined to by the sum of the entries on the main diagonal of AAA﻿.
    • Let u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ be column vectors of any size. The product uTv\textbf{u}^\mathsf{T}\textbf{v}uTv﻿ is called the matrix inner product of u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿.
    • let u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ be column vectors with the same size. Then
  • Theorems
    • (Linearity Properties) Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. Let u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ be column vectors in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn﻿ and ccc﻿ be a scalar. Then the following hold.
      1. A(cu)=c(Au)A(c\textbf{u}) = c(A\textbf{u})A(cu)=c(Au)﻿
      1. A(u+v)=Au+AvA(\textbf{u}+\textbf{v}) = A\textbf{u}+A\textbf{v}A(u+v)=Au+Av﻿
      • proof

        Let Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ be a linear system. Then exactly one of the following is true: (a) the system has no solutions, (b) the system has exactly one solution, or (c) the system has more than one solution. Hence it suffices to show that the system has infinitely many solutions in case (c).

        Let x1x_1x1​﻿ and x2x_2x2​﻿ be two different solutions of the linear system so that Ax1=bAx_1 = bAx1​=b﻿ and Ax2=bAx_2 = bAx2​=b﻿. Then A(x1−x2)=0A(x_1-x_2)=0A(x1​−x2​)=0﻿. Letting x0=x1−x2x_0=x_1-x_2x0​=x1​−x2​﻿, we have for any scalar ccc﻿,

        which implies that x1+cx0x_1+cx_0 x1​+cx0​﻿ is a solution of the linear system. Thus the system Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ has infinityly many solutions. □\square□﻿

    • (The Row-Column Rule or Dot Product Rule). Let AA A﻿ be an m×sm\times sm×s﻿ matrix and BBB﻿ be an s×ns\times ns×n﻿ matrix. Then for i=1,2,...,mi=1,2,...,m i=1,2,...,m﻿ and j=1,2,...,nj=1,2,...,nj=1,2,...,n﻿, the entry in the iii﻿th row and jj j﻿th column of the matrix product ABABAB﻿ is the iii﻿th row vector of AA A﻿ times the jj j﻿th column vector of BBB﻿, or equivalently, the dot product of the iii﻿th row vector of AA A﻿ and the jj j﻿th column vector of BBB﻿, i.e.,

      where aika_{ik}aik​﻿ is the ikikik﻿th entry of AAA﻿ and bkjb_{kj} bkj​﻿ is the kjkjkj﻿th entry of BBB﻿ for k=1,2,...,s.k=1,2,...,s.k=1,2,...,s.﻿

    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×sm\times s m×s﻿ matrix whose row vectors are a1,a2,...,ama_1, a_2, ..., a_ma1​,a2​,...,am​﻿ and BBB﻿ be an s×ns\times ns×n﻿ matrix whose column vectors are b1,b2,...,bnb_1, b_2, ..., b_nb1​,b2​,...,bn​﻿. Then the following hold.
      1. For j=1,2,...,nj = 1,2,...,nj=1,2,...,n﻿, the jjj﻿th column vector of the matrix product ABABAB﻿ is a linear combination of the column vectors of AAA﻿ with the coefficients coming from bjb_jbj​﻿.
      1. For i=1,2,...,mi = 1,2,...,mi=1,2,...,m﻿, the iii﻿th row vector of the matrix product ABABAB﻿ is a linear combination of the row vectors of BBB﻿ with the coefficients coming from aia_iai​﻿.

3.2 Inverses; Algebraic Properties of Matrices

  • Definitions
    • The negative or the additive inverse of a matrix AA A﻿ is defined to be
    • A matrix whose entries are all zero is called a zero matrix and is denoted by OOO﻿.
    • A square matrix with 1's on the main diagonal and 0's off the main diagonal is called an identity matrix and is denoted by III﻿. The n×nn\times nn×n﻿ identity matrix is denoted by InI_nIn​﻿ if it is important to emphasize the size.
    • An n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix AA A﻿ to the zeroth power is defined to be InI_nIn​﻿, i.e.,
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix. If there is a matrix BBB﻿ with the same size as AAA﻿ such that

      then AA A﻿ is said to be invertible (or nonsingular) and BBB﻿ is called an inverse of AAA﻿. If there is no matrix BBB﻿ with this property, then AAA﻿ is said to be singular (or noninvertible).

    • Let AA A﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix and

      be a polynomial. Define the n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix p(A)p(A)p(A)﻿ to be

      that is, p(A)p(A)p(A)﻿ is obtained by substituting AAA﻿ for xxx﻿ and replacing the constant term a0a_0a0​﻿ by the matrix a0Ina_0I_na0​In​﻿. An expression of form is called a matrix polynomial in AAA﻿.

  • Theorems
    • Let aaa﻿ and bb b﻿ be any scalars. Let AAA﻿, BBB﻿ and CC C﻿ be matrices with the same size. Then the following rules of matrix arithmatic are valid.
      1. (Commutative law for addition) A+B=B+AA+B=B+AA+B=B+A﻿
      1. (Associative law for addition) A+(B+C)=(A+B)+CA+(B+C) = (A+B )+CA+(B+C)=(A+B)+C﻿
      1. (Associative law of scalar multiplication) (ab)A=a(bA)(ab)A=a(bA)(ab)A=a(bA)﻿
      1. (Right distributive law) (a+b)A=aA+bA(a+b)A = aA + bA(a+b)A=aA+bA﻿
      1. (Right distributive law) (a−b)A=aA−bA(a-b)A = aA - bA(a−b)A=aA−bA﻿
      1. (Left distributive law) a(A+B)=aA+aBa(A+B) = aA + aBa(A+B)=aA+aB﻿
      1. (Left distributive law) a(A−B)=aA−bBa(A-B) = aA - bBa(A−B)=aA−bB﻿
    • Let aaa﻿ be any scalar. Let AAA﻿, BBB﻿ and CC C﻿ be matrices. Assuming that the sizes of the matrices are such that the indicated operations can be performed, the following rules of matrix arithmetic are valid.
      1. (Associative law for multiplication) A(BC)=(AB)CA(BC)=(AB)CA(BC)=(AB)C﻿
      1. (Right distributive law) (B+C)A=BA+CA(B+C)A = BA + CA(B+C)A=BA+CA﻿
      1. (Right distributive law) (B−C)A=BA−CA(B-C)A = BA - CA(B−C)A=BA−CA﻿
      1. (Left distributive law) A(B+C)=AB+ACA(B+C) = AB + ACA(B+C)=AB+AC﻿
      1. (Left distributive law) A(B−C)=AB−ACA(B-C) = AB - ACA(B−C)=AB−AC﻿
      1. a(BC)=(aB)C=B(aC)a(BC)=(aB)C=B(aC)a(BC)=(aB)C=B(aC)﻿
    • Let RRR﻿ be the reduced row echelon form of an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then either RRR﻿ has a row of zeros or RRR﻿ is the identity matrix InI_nIn​﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an invertible matrix. If BBB﻿ and CCC﻿ are both inverses of AAA﻿, then B=CB=CB=C﻿. That is, an invertible matrix has a unique inverse.
    • Let AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ be invertible matrices of the same size. Then ABABAB﻿ is invertible and
    • Let AAA﻿ be an invertible matrix. Then the following hold.
      1. A−1A^{-1}A−1﻿ is invertible and
      1. Let mmm﻿ be a nonnegative integer. Then AmA^mAm﻿ is invertible and
      1. Let kkk﻿ be a nonzero scalar. Then kAkAkA﻿ is invertible and
      1. Let rrr﻿ and sss﻿ be integers. Then
    • Let kkk﻿ be any scalar. Let AAA﻿ and BB B﻿ be matrices. Assuming that the sizes of the matrices are such that the indicated operations can be performed, the following hold.
      1. (AT)T=A(A^\mathsf{T})^\mathsf{T}=A(AT)T=A﻿
      1. (A+B)T=AT+BT(A+B)^\mathsf{T} = A^\mathsf{T} + B^\mathsf{T}(A+B)T=AT+BT﻿
      1. (A−B)T=AT−BT(A-B)^\mathsf{T} = A^\mathsf{T} - B^\mathsf{T}(A−B)T=AT−BT﻿
      1. (kA)T=kAT(kA)^\mathsf{T} = k A^\mathsf{T}(kA)T=kAT﻿
      1. (AB)T=BTAT(AB)^\mathsf{T} = B^\mathsf{T}A^\mathsf{T}(AB)T=BTAT﻿
    • Let AA A﻿ be an invertible matrix. Then ATA^\mathsf{T}AT﻿ is invertible and
    • Let kk k﻿ be any scalar. Let AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ be square matrices with the same sizse. Then the following hold.
      1. tr(AT)=tr(A)tr(A^\mathsf{T}) = tr(A)tr(AT)=tr(A)﻿
      1. tr(kA)=ktr(A)tr(kA) = ktr(A)tr(kA)=ktr(A)﻿
      1. tr(A+B)=tr(A)+tr(B)tr(A+B) = tr(A) + tr(B)tr(A+B)=tr(A)+tr(B)﻿
      1. tr(A−B)=tr(A)−tr(B)tr(A-B) = tr(A) - tr(B)tr(A−B)=tr(A)−tr(B)﻿
      1. tr(AB)=tr(BA)tr(AB) = tr(BA)tr(AB)=tr(BA)﻿
    • Let r\textbf{r}r﻿ be a 1×n1\times n1×n﻿ row vector and ccc﻿ be an n×1n\times 1n×1﻿ column vector. Then

3.3 Elementary Matrices; A Method for Finding A−1A^{-1}A−1﻿

  • Definitions
    • An n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix is called an elementary matrix if it can be obtained from the n×nn\times nn×n﻿ identity matrix InI_nIn​﻿ by performing a single elementary row operation.
  • Theorems
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. If an elementary matrix EEE﻿ results from performing a certain row operation on ImI_mIm​﻿, then the product EAEAEA﻿ is the matrix that results when this same operation is performed on AAA﻿.
    • Every elementary matrix is invertible and the inverse is an elementary matrix.
      • proof

        Let EE E﻿ be an elementary matrix. Then EEE﻿ results from performing some row operation on III﻿. Let E0E_0E0​﻿ be the matrix that results when the inverse of this operation is performed on III﻿. Applying the theorem and using the fact taht inverse row operations cancel the effect of each other, we have

        Thus the elementary matrix E0E_0 E0​﻿ is the inverse of EEE﻿. □\square□﻿

    • Let AA A﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. The reduced row echelon form of AAA﻿ is InI_nIn​﻿.
      1. AAA﻿ is expressible as a product of elementary matrices.
      1. AAA﻿ is invertible.
      • proof

        First, let InI_nIn​﻿ be the reduced row echelon form of AAA﻿ so that AAA﻿ is reduced to InI_nIn​﻿ by a finite sequence of elementary row operations. Each of these operations can be accomplished by multiplying on the left by an appropriate elementary matrix. Thus there exist elementary matrices E1,E2,...,EkE_1, E_2,...,E_kE1​,E2​,...,Ek​﻿ such that

        Each EiE_iEi​﻿ is invertible and Ei−1E_i^{-1}Ei−1​﻿ is an elementary matrix. Then

        Therefore (i) implies (ii). Also (ii) implies (iii).

        Now, let AA A﻿ be invertible and RRR﻿ be the reduced row echelon form of AAA﻿. Then there exist elementary row matrices E1,E2,...,EkE_1,E_2,...,E_kE1​,E2​,...,Ek​﻿ such that

        Thus RRR﻿ is invertible. Since RRR﻿ cannot have a row of zeros, it follows that R=InR=I_nR=In​﻿. Therefore (iii) implies (i). □\square□﻿

    • Let AA A﻿ and BB B﻿ be square matrices of the same size. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ are row equivalent.
      1. There is an invertible matrix EE E﻿ such that B=EAB=EAB=EA﻿.
      1. There is an invertible matrix FFF﻿ such that A=FBA=FBA=FB﻿.
    • Let Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ be a linear system of nnn﻿ equations in nnn﻿ unknowns. If the coefficient matrix AAA﻿ is invertible, then the system has exactly one solution, namely
    • Let Ax=0Ax=0 Ax=0﻿ be a homogeneous linear system of nnn﻿ equations in nnn﻿ unknowns. Then the system has only the trivial solution if and only if the coefficient matrix AAA﻿ is invertible.
    • Let AA A﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. The reduced row echelon form of AA A﻿ is InI_nIn​﻿.
      1. AAA﻿ is expressible as a product of elementary matrices.
      1. AAA﻿ is invertible.
      1. Ax=0Ax=0 Ax=0﻿ has only the trivial solution.
    • Let AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ be square matrices. Then the following hold.
      1. If AB=1AB=1AB=1﻿ or BA=IBA=IBA=I﻿, then AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ are both invertible and each is the inverse of the other.
      1. If ABABAB﻿ is invertible, then AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ are invertible.
    • Let AA A﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. The reduced row echelon form of AAA﻿ is InI_nIn​﻿.
      1. AAA﻿ is expressible as a product of elementary matrices.
      1. AAA﻿ is invertible.
      1. Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ has only the trivial solution.
      1. Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ is consistent for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. Ax=bAx=b Ax=b﻿ has exactly one solution for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
  • The Inversion Algorithm

    Let AA A﻿ be a square matrix. To find A−1A^{-1}A−1﻿, if it exits, preceed as follows:

    Step 1

    Form the augmented matrix [A∣I][A\mid I][A∣I]﻿.

    Step 2

    Apply Gauss-Jordan elimination to attempt to reduce [A∣I][A\mid I] [A∣I]﻿ to [I∣B][I\mid B][I∣B]﻿. If the reduction can be carried out, then A−1=BA^{-1}=BA−1=B﻿. Otherwise, A−1A^{-1}A−1﻿ does not exist.

3.4 Subspaces and Linear Independence

  • Definitions
    • A nonempty set WW W﻿ of vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿ is called a subspace of Rn\R^nRn﻿ if it is closed under scalar multiplication (i.e. any scalar multiple of a vector in WW W﻿ is in WWW﻿) and is closed under addition (i.e. the sum of any two vectors in WW W﻿ is in WWW﻿).
    • The subspace WWW﻿ of all linear combinations

      is denoted by

      The vectors v1,v2,...,vk\textbf{v}_1,\textbf{v}_2,...,\textbf{v}_k v1​,v2​,...,vk​﻿ are said to span (or generate) WWW﻿. The set {v1,v2,...,vk}\{ \textbf{v}_1,\textbf{v}_2,...,\textbf{v}_k \}{v1​,v2​,...,vk​}﻿ is said to span WWW﻿.

    • The set consisting of the zero vector in Rn\R^nRn﻿ is called the zero subspace of Rn\R^nRn﻿. The space Rn\R^nRn﻿ and the zero subspace of Rn\R^nRn﻿ are called the trivial subspaces of Rn\R^nRn﻿.
    • Let S={v1,v2,...,vk}S = \{\textbf{v}_1,\textbf{v}_2,...,\textbf{v}_k \}S={v1​,v2​,...,vk​}﻿ be a nonempty set of vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿. Then SSS﻿ is said to be linear independent if the only scalars c1,c2,...,csc_1,c_2,...,c_sc1​,c2​,...,cs​﻿ that satisfy the equation

      are

      that is, the zero vector is a unique linear combination of v1,v2,...,vs\textbf{v}_1,\textbf{v}_2,...,\textbf{v}_sv1​,v2​,...,vs​﻿ (or the vectors v1,v2,...,vs\textbf{v}_1,\textbf{v}_2,...,\textbf{v}_sv1​,v2​,...,vs​﻿ are said to be linear independent). If there are scalars, not all zeros, that satisfy this equation, the SSS﻿ is said to be linearly dependent. (or the vectors v1,v2,...,vs\textbf{v}_1,\textbf{v}_2,...,\textbf{v}_sv1​,v2​,...,vs​﻿ are said to be linearly dependent).

    • let WW W﻿be a subspace of Rn\R^nRn﻿ and x0x_0x0​﻿ be a vector in Rn\R^nRn﻿. The translation of WWW﻿by x0x_0x0​﻿ is defined to be the set of vectors of the form x=x0+vx=x_0 + \textbf{v}x=x0​+v﻿ with a vector v\textbf{v}v﻿ in WWW﻿ and is denoted by x0+Wx_0 + Wx0​+W﻿.
  • Theorems
    • Let v1,v2,...,vk\textbf{v}_1, \textbf{v}_2,..., \textbf{v}_kv1​,v2​,...,vk​﻿ be vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿. Then the set of all linear combinations

      is a subspace of Rn\R^nRn﻿.

    • Let Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ be a homogeneous linear system with nnn﻿ unknowns. Then its solution set is a subspace of Rn\R^nRn﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ be matrices with nnn﻿ comlumns. Then the following hold.
      1. The solution space of the homogeneous linear system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ is all of Rn\R^nRn﻿ if and only if A=OA=OA=O﻿.
      1. A=BA=B A=B﻿ if and only if Ax=BxAx=BxAx=Bx﻿ for every vector xx x﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
    • A set S={v1,v2,...,vs}S=\{ \textbf{v}_1,\textbf{v}_2,...,\textbf{v}_s \}S={v1​,v2​,...,vs​}﻿ with two or more vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿ is linearly dependent if and only if at least one of the vectors in SSS﻿ is expressible as a linear combination of the other vectors in SSS﻿.
    • A homogeneous linear system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ has only the trivial solution if and only if the column vectors of AAA﻿ are linearly independent.
    • A set with more than nnn﻿ vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿ is linearly dependent.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. The reduced row echelon form of AAA﻿ is InI_nIn​﻿.
      1. AAA﻿ is expressible as a product of elementary matrices.
      1. AAA﻿ is invertible.
      1. Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ has only the trivial solution.
      1. Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ is consistent for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. Ax=bAx=b Ax=b﻿ has exactly one solution for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. The column vectors of AAA﻿ are linearly independent.
      1. The row vectors of AAA﻿ are linearly independent.

3.5 The Geometry of Linear Systems

  • Definitions
    • The solution set of a consistent nonhomogeneous linear system Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ is expressible in the form

      where x0x_0x0​﻿ is any solution of the nonhomogeneous linear system Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ and

      is a general solution of the associated homogeneous linear system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿. The solution x0x_0x0​﻿ is called a particular solution of Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿. The solution set in the form is called a general solution of Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿.

    • Let a=(a1,a2,...,an)a=(a_1,a_2,...,a_n)a=(a1​,a2​,...,an​)﻿ be a nonzero vector in Rn\R^nRn﻿. The hyperplane through the origin of Rn\R^nRn﻿ with normal aaa﻿ (or the orthogonal complement of aaa﻿), denoted a⊥a^\bota⊥﻿, is defined to be the set of all points x=(x1,x2,...,xn)x=(x_1,x_2,...,x_n)x=(x1​,x2​,...,xn​)﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿ that satisfy the linear equation

      This linear equation can be expressed in dot product notation as a⋅x=0a\cdot x=0a⋅x=0﻿.

    • Let a=(a1,a2,...,an)a=(a_1,a_2,...,a_n)a=(a1​,a2​,...,an​)﻿ be a nonzero vector in Rn\R^nRn﻿ and bb b﻿ be a scalar. The set of all points x=(x1,x2,...,xn)x=(x_1,x_2,...,x_n)x=(x1​,x2​,...,xn​)﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿ that satisfy the linear equation

      is called a hyperplane in Rn\R^nRn﻿. This linear equation can be expressed in dot product notation as a⋅x=ba\cdot x=ba⋅x=b﻿

  • Theorems
    • Let Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ be a consistent nonhomogeneous linear system. If WWW﻿ is the solution space of the associated homogeneous linear system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿, then the solution set of Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ is the translated subspace x0+Wx_0 + Wx0​+W﻿ where x0x_0x0​﻿ is any solution of the nonhomogeneous linear system Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿.
    • A general solution of a consistent linear system Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ can be obtained by adding a particular solution of Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ to a general solution of the associated homogeneous linear system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿.
    • Let AA A﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ has only the trivial solution.
      1. Ax=bAx=b Ax=b﻿ has at most one solution for every vector bbb﻿ in Rm\R^mRm﻿ (i.e. is inconsistent or has a unique solution).
    • A nonhomogeneous linear system with more unknowns than equations is either inconsistent or has infinitely many solutions.
    • A linear system Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ is consistent if and only if bbb﻿ is in the space spanned by the column vectors of AAA﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. Then the solution space of the homogeneous linear system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ consists of all vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿ that are orthogonal to every row vector of the matrix AAA﻿.

3.6 Matrices with Special Forms

  • Definitions
    • A square matrix in which all entries off the main diagonal are zero is called a diagonal matrix.
    • A square matrix in which all entries above the main diagonal are zero is called lower triangular. A square matrix in which all entries below the main diagonal are zero is called upper triangle. A matrix that is either upper triangular or lower triangular (or both) is called triangular.
    • A lower triangular matrix with zeros on the main diagonal is called strictly lower triangular. An upper triangular matrix with zeros on the main diagonal is called strictly upper triangular. A matrix that is either strictly upper triangular or strictly lower triangular (or both) is called strictly triangular.
    • A square matrix AAA﻿ is said to be symmetric if

      It is said to be skew-symmetric if

    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. A vector x0x_0x0​﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿ is called a fixed point of AAA﻿ if

      or equivalently, x0x_0x0​﻿ is a solution of the homogeneous linear system (I−A)x=0(I-A)x=0(I−A)x=0﻿.

    • A square matrix AAA﻿ with the property that Ak=OA^k=OAk=O﻿ for some positive integer kkk﻿ is said to be nilpotent and the smallest positive integer power kkk﻿ for which Ak=OA^k=OAk=O﻿ is called the index of nilpotent.
  • Theorems
    • The following statement hold.
      1. The transpose of a lower triangular matrix is upper triangular and the transpose of an upper triangular matrix is lower triangular.
      1. A product of lower triangular matrices is lower triangular and a product of upper triangular matrices is upper triangular.
      1. A triangular matrix is invertible if and only if its diagonal entries are all nonzero.
      1. The inverse of an invertible lower triangular matrix is lower triangular and the inverse of an invertible upper triangular matrix is upper triangular.
    • Let AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ be symmetric matrices with the same size and kkk﻿ be any scalar. Then the following hold.
      1. ATA^\mathsf{T}AT﻿ is symmetric.
      1. A+BA+BA+B﻿ and A−BA-BA−B﻿ are symmetric.
      1. kAkAkA﻿ is symmetric.
    • The product of two symmetric matrices is symmetric if and only if the matrices commute.
    • The inverse of an invertible symmetric matrix is symmetric.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix. Then the matrix A,AATA, AA^\mathsf{T}A,AAT﻿ and ATAA^\mathsf{T}AATA﻿ are either all invertible or all singular.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix such that there is a positive integer kkk﻿ for which Ak=OA^k=OAk=O﻿. Then the matrix I−AI-AI−A﻿ is invertible and
    • Let AA A﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix for which the sum of the absolute values of the entries in each column (or each row) is less than 1. Then the matrix I−AI-AI−A﻿ is invertible and can be expressed as power series representation

3.7 Matrix Factorizations; LU\rm{\textit{LU}}LU﻿-Decomposition

  • Definitions
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix. A factorization of AAA﻿ as

      where LLL﻿ is a lower triangular matrix and UUU﻿ is an upper triangular matrix is called an LULULU﻿-decomposition or LULULU﻿-factorization of AAA﻿.

    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. For k=1,2,...,n,k=1,2,...,n,k=1,2,...,n,﻿ the upper leftmost k×kk\times kk×k﻿ submatrix of AAA﻿, i.e., the matrix of AA A﻿ with the last n−kn-kn−k﻿ rows and columns removed, is called a leading principal submatrix of AAA﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix. A factorization of AA A﻿ as

      where LLL﻿ is a lower triangular matrix with 1's on the main diagonal, DDD﻿ is a diagonal matrix and UUU﻿ is an upper triangular matrix with 1's on the main diagonal is called LDULDULDU﻿-decomposition or LDULDULDU﻿-factorization of AAA﻿.

    • An n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix PPP﻿ is called a permutation matrix if it results by reordering the rows of InI_nIn​﻿ in some way, i.e., it is formed by multiplying in sequence those elementary matrices that correspond to the row interchanges.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix. A factorization of AA A﻿ as

      where PPP﻿ is a permutation matrix, LLL﻿ is a lower triangular matrix and UUU﻿ is an upper triangular matrix is called a PLUPLUPLU﻿-decomposition or PLUPLUPLU﻿-factorization of AAA﻿.

  • Theorems
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix. If AAA﻿ can be reduced to row echelon form by Gaussian elimination with no row interchanges, then AAA﻿ has an LULULU﻿-decomposition.
  • The Method of LULULU﻿-Decomposition

    Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix in the form A=LUA=LUA=LU﻿ where LLL﻿ is a lower triangular matrix and UUU﻿ is an upper triangular matrix. To solve a linear system Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿, proceed as follows:

    Step 1. Rewrite the system Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ as

    Step 2. Define a new unknown yyy﻿ by letting

    and rewrite the equation as Ly=bLy=bLy=b﻿

    Step 3. Solve the linear system Ly=bLy=bLy=b﻿ for the unknown yyy﻿.

    Step 4. Substitute the now-known vector yyy﻿ into this equation and solve for xxx﻿.

  • Finding an LULULU﻿-Decomposition of a matrix

    Step 1. Reduce AAA﻿ to row echelon form UUU﻿ without using row interchanges, keeping track of the multipliers used to introduce the leading 1's and the multiplers used to introduce zeros below the leading 1's.

    Step 2. In each position below the main diagonal of LLL﻿, place the reciprocal of the multipliers that introduced the leading 1 in that position in UUU﻿.

    Step 3. In each position along the main diagonal of LLL﻿, place the negative of the multipliers used to introduce the zero in that position in UUU﻿.

    Step 4. Form the decomposition A=LUA=LUA=LU﻿.

4.1 Determinants; Cofactor Expansion

  • Definitions
    • Let AAA﻿ be a 2×22\times 22×2﻿ or 3×33\times 33×3﻿ matrix. The determinant of AAA﻿, denoted det(A)\textrm{det}(A)det(A)﻿ or ∣A∣|A|∣A∣﻿, is defined as follows:
      1. 2×22\times 22×2﻿ case: If

        then

      2. 3×33\times 33×3﻿ case: If

      then

    • A permutation of the set of integers from 1 to nnn﻿ is an arrangement of these integers in some order without omissions or repetitions. An inversion is said to occur in a permutation whenever a larger integer precedes a smaller one.
    • A permutation is called even if the total number of inversions is an even integer. It is called odd if the total number of inversions is an odd integer. That is, a permutation is defined to be even or odd in accordance with whether the minimum number of interchanges required to put the permutation in natural order is even or odd.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. An elementary product from AAA﻿ is defined to be a product of nnn﻿ entries from AAA﻿, no two of which come from the same row or same column. If A=(aij)A=(a_{ij})A=(aij​)﻿, then each elementary product is expressible in the form

      where the column indices form a permutation (j1,j2,...,jn)(j_1,j_2,...,j_n)(j1​,j2​,...,jn​)﻿ of the set of integers from 1 to nnn﻿ and the row indices are in natural order. The signed elementary product (from AAA﻿) corresponding to the form is defined to be

      where the sign is +++﻿ if the permutation (j1,j2,...,jn)(j_1,j_2,...,j_n)(j1​,j2​,...,jn​)﻿ is even and −-−﻿ if it is odd.

    • The determinant of a square matrix AAA﻿ is defined to be the sum of all signed elementary products from AA A﻿ and is denoted by det(A)\textrm{det}(A)det(A)﻿ or ∣A∣|A|∣A∣﻿.
    • Let A=(aij)A=(a_{ij})A=(aij​)﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. For i,j=1,2,...,n,i,j=1,2,...,n, i,j=1,2,...,n,﻿ the minor of entry aija_{ij}aij​﻿ (or the ijijij﻿th minor of AAA﻿) is denoted by MijM_{ij}Mij​﻿ and is defined to be the determinant of the (n−1)×(n−1)(n-1)\times (n-1)(n−1)×(n−1)﻿ submatrix that remains when the iii﻿th row and jjj﻿th column of AAA﻿ are deleted. The number

      is called the cofactor of entry aija_{ij}aij​﻿ (or the ijijij﻿th cofactor of AAA﻿).

  • Theorems
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix with a row of zeros or a column of zeros. Then det(A)=0\textrm{det}(A)=0det(A)=0﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a triangular matrix. Then det(A)\textrm{det}(A)det(A)﻿ is the product of the entries on the main diagonal.
    • Let A=(aij)A=(a_{ij})A=(aij​)﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then det(A)\textrm{det}(A)det(A)﻿ can be computed by multiplying the entries in any row (or column) by their cofactors and adding the resulting products, that is, for each i=1,2,...,n,i=1,2,...,n,i=1,2,...,n,﻿

      and for each j=1,2,...,n,j=1,2,...,n,j=1,2,...,n,﻿

4.2 Properties of Determinants

  • Theorems
    • Let AA A﻿ be a square matrix. Then det(A)=det(AT)\textrm{det}(A) = \textrm{det}(A^\mathsf{T})det(A)=det(AT)﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix. Then the following hold. (determinant equalities)
      1. If BBB﻿ is the matrix that results when a single row or a single column of AA A﻿ is multiplied by a scalar kkk﻿, then
      1. If BBB﻿ is the matrix that results when two rows or two columns of AAA﻿ are interchanged, then
      1. If BBB﻿ is the matrix that results when a multiple of one row of AAA﻿ is added to another row or when a multiple of one column of AA A﻿ is added to another column, then
    • Let AA A﻿ be a square matrix. Then the following hold. (etc properties)
      1. If AAA﻿ has two identical rows or columns, then det(A)=0\textrm{det}(A)=0det(A)=0﻿.
      1. If AAA﻿ has two proportional rows or columns, then det(A)=0\textrm{det}(A)=0det(A)=0﻿.
      1. If AAA﻿ is an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix and k∈Rk\in \Rk∈R﻿, then
    • A square matrix AAA﻿ is invertible if and only if det(A)≠0\textrm{det}(A)\neq 0det(A)​=0﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ and BBB﻿ be square matrices of the same size. Then
    • Let AAA﻿ be an invertible matrix. Then
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. The reduced row echelon form of AAA﻿ is InI_nIn​﻿.
      1. AAA﻿ is expressible as a product of elementary matrices.
      1. AAA﻿ is invertible.
      1. Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ has only the trivial solution.
      1. Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ has consistent for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ has exactly one solution for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. The column vectors of AA A﻿ are linearly independent.
      1. The row vectors of AAA﻿ are linearly independet.
      1. det(A)≠0.\textrm{det}(A)\neq0.det(A)​=0.﻿
  • Computation of a Determinant
    1. Reduce AAA﻿ to a triangular matrix, using only row addition and row interchange.
    1. If any of the matrices appearing in the reduction contains a row of zeros, then det(A)=0\textrm{det}(A)=0det(A)=0﻿.
    1. Otherwise, det(A)=(−1)r⋅(Product of diagonal entries)\textrm{det}(A)=(-1)^r\cdot(\textrm{Product of diagonal entries})det(A)=(−1)r⋅(Product of diagonal entries)﻿ where rrr﻿ is the number of row interchanges performed.

4.3 Cramer's Rule; Formula for A−1A^{-1}A−1﻿; Applications of Determinants

  • Definitions
    • Let A=(aij)A=(a_{ij})A=(aij​)﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix and CijC_{ij}Cij​﻿ be the cofactors of entries aija_{ij}aij​﻿ for i,j=1,2,3,...,ni,j=1,2,3,...,ni,j=1,2,3,...,n﻿. The matrix

      is called the matrix of cofactors from AAA﻿. The transpose of this matrix is called the adjoint (or the adjudate) of AAA﻿ and is denoted by adj(A)\textrm{adj}(A)adj(A)﻿. That is,

    • Let u=(u1,u2,u3)\textbf{u}=(u_1,u_2,u_3)u=(u1​,u2​,u3​)﻿ and v=(v1,v2,v3)\textbf{v} = (v_1,v_2,v_3)v=(v1​,v2​,v3​)﻿ be vectors in R3\R^3R3﻿. The cross product of u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿, denoted by u×v\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}u×v﻿, is defined to be the vector in R3\R^3R3﻿ given by

      or equivalently, in determinant notation

      Symbolically,

      where i=(1,0,0)\textbf{i} = (1,0,0)i=(1,0,0)﻿, j=(0,1,0)\textbf{j} = (0,1,0)j=(0,1,0)﻿ and k=(0,0,1)\textbf{k} = (0,0,1)k=(0,0,1)﻿ are the standard unit vectors in R3\R^3R3﻿.

    • Let u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ be vectors in R3\R^3R3﻿. The cross product of u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿, denoted u×v\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}u×v﻿, is defined to be the vector in R3\R^3R3﻿ whose length and direction are given as follows
      • The length of u×v\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}u×v﻿ is the area of the parallelogram determined by u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ if u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ are not parallel. It is zero if u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ are parallel. Alternatively,

        where θ\thetaθ﻿ is the angle between u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ in [0,π][0,\pi][0,π]﻿ if u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ are nonzero vectors and θ\thetaθ﻿ is any angle if either u\textbf{u}u﻿ or v\textbf{v}v﻿ (or both) is 0.

      • The direction of u×v\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}u×v﻿ is such that u×v\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}u×v﻿ is orthogonal to both u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ and the ordered triple (u\textbf{u}u﻿, v\textbf{v}v﻿, u×v\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}u×v﻿) is a right-handed set of vectors if u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ are not parallel.

  • Theorems
    • Let AAA﻿ be a square matrix. Then the following hold.
      1. If the entries in any row of AAA﻿ are multiplied by the cofactors of the corresponding entries in a different row, then the sum of the products is zero.
      1. If the entries in any column of AAA﻿ are multiplied by the cofactors of the corresponding entries in a different column, then the sum of the products is zero.

    • Let AA A﻿ be an invertible matrix. Then
    • Let Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ be a linear system of nnn﻿ equations in nnn﻿ unknowns. Then the system has a unique solution if and only if det(A)≠0\textrm{det}(A)\neq0det(A)​=0﻿, in which case the solution is

      where AjA_jAj​﻿ is the matrix that results when the jjj﻿th column of AAA﻿ is replaced by bbb﻿ for each j=1,2,...,nj = 1,2,...,nj=1,2,...,n﻿.

    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then the following hold.
      1. If n=2n=2n=2﻿, then ∣det(A)∣|\textrm{det}(A)|∣det(A)∣﻿ represents the area of the parallelogram determined by the two column vectors of AAA﻿ when they are positioned so their initial points coincide.
      1. If n=3n=3n=3﻿, then ∣det(A)∣|\textrm{det}(A)|∣det(A)∣﻿ represents the volume of the parallelogram determined by the three column vectors of AAA﻿ when they are positioned so their initial points coincide.
    • Let SSS﻿ be a triangle in the xyxyxy﻿-plane with vertices P1(x1,y1)P_1(x_1,y_1)P1​(x1​,y1​)﻿, P2(x2,y2)P_2(x_2,y_2)P2​(x2​,y2​)﻿ and P3(x3,y3)P_3(x_3,y_3)P3​(x3​,y3​)﻿ where the labeling is such that the triangle is traversed counterclockwise from P1P_1P1​﻿ to P2P_2P2​﻿ to P3P_3P3​﻿. Then the area of SSS﻿ is given by

    • Let u\textbf{u}u﻿, v\textbf{v}v﻿ and w\textbf{w}w﻿ be vectors in R3\R^3R3﻿ and kkk﻿ be a scalar. Then the following hold
      1. (Anticommutativity) u×v=−v×u\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v} = - \textbf{v} \times \textbf{u}u×v=−v×u﻿
      1. (Distributivity) u×(v+w)=u×v+u×w\textbf{u}\times (\textbf{v} + \textbf{w} ) = \textbf{u}\times \textbf{v} + \textbf{u}\times \textbf{w}u×(v+w)=u×v+u×w﻿
      1. (Distributivity) (u+v)×w=u×w+v×w(\textbf{u}+ \textbf{v}) \times \textbf{w} = \textbf{u}\times \textbf{w} + \textbf{v}\times \textbf{w}(u+v)×w=u×w+v×w﻿
      1. k(u×v)=(ku)×v=u×(kv)k(\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}) = (k\textbf{u})\times \textbf{v} = \textbf{u}\times (k\textbf{v})k(u×v)=(ku)×v=u×(kv)﻿
      1. u×0=0×u=0\textbf{u}\times 0 = 0\times \textbf{u} =0u×0=0×u=0﻿
      1. u×u=0\textbf{u}\times \textbf{u}=0u×u=0﻿
    • Let u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ be vectors in R3\R^3R3﻿. Then the following hold.
      1. u⋅(u×v)=0\textbf{u}\cdot(\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}) =0 u⋅(u×v)=0﻿, i.e., u×v\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}u×v﻿ is orthogonal to u\textbf{u}u﻿.
      1. v⋅(u×v)=0\textbf{v}\cdot(\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}) =0 v⋅(u×v)=0﻿, i.e., u×v\textbf{u}\times \textbf{v}u×v﻿ is orthogonal to v\textbf{v}v﻿.
    • Let u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ be nonzero vectors in R3\R^3R3﻿ and θ\thetaθ﻿ be the angle between u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ in [0,π][0,\pi][0,π]﻿. Then the following hold.
      1. (Lagrange's Identity) ∥u×v∥2=∥u∥2∥v∥2−(u⋅v)2,i.e.,\lVert \textbf{u}\times \textbf{v} \lVert^2 = \lVert\textbf{u}\lVert^2\lVert\textbf{v}\lVert^2 - (\textbf{u}\cdot \textbf{v})^2, i.e.,∥u×v∥2=∥u∥2∥v∥2−(u⋅v)2,i.e.,﻿
      1. The area of the parallelogram that has u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ as adjacent side is

4.4 A First Look at Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

  • Definitions
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. A scalar λ\lambdaλ﻿ is called an eigenvalue of AAA﻿ if there is a nonzero vector xxx﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿ such that

      If λ\lambdaλ﻿ is an eigenvalue of AAA﻿, then every nonzero vector xxx﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿ such that Ax=λxAx=\lambda xAx=λx﻿ is called an eigenvector of AAA﻿ corresponding to λ\lambdaλ﻿. The terms characteristic value or proper value are used in place of eigenvalue. The terms characteristic vector or proper vector are used in place eigenvector.

    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix and λ\lambda λ﻿ be a scalar. The equation

      is called the characteristic equation of AAA﻿. The determinant det(λIn−A)\textrm{det}(\lambda I_n-A)det(λIn​−A)﻿ is a polynomial in λ\lambda λ﻿ called the characteristic polynomial of AAA﻿. It has the form

    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix and λ\lambdaλ﻿ be an eigenvalue of AAA﻿. The solution space of the homogeneous linear system det(λIn−A)=0\textrm{det}(\lambda I_n-A) = 0det(λIn​−A)=0﻿ is called the eigenspace of AAA﻿ corresponding to λ\lambdaλ﻿. That is, the eigenspace of AAA﻿ is the set consisting of the zero vector in Rn\R^nRn﻿ together with all eigenvectors of AAA﻿ corresponding to λ\lambdaλ﻿. It is a subspace of Rn\R^nRn﻿.
    • For i=1,2,⋯ ,ni=1,2,\cdots,ni=1,2,⋯,n﻿, the exponent mim_imi​﻿ is called the algebraic multiplicity of λi\lambda_iλi​﻿. That is, the algebraic multiplicity of an eigenvalue of AAA﻿ is its multiplicity as a root of the characteristic equation of AAA﻿. The expression is called the complete linear factorization of the characteristic polynomial.
  • Theorems
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. AAA﻿ has nontrivial fixed points.
      1. In−AI_n - AIn​−A﻿ is singular.
      1. det(In−A)=0\textrm{det}(I_n-A) = 0det(In​−A)=0﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix and λ\lambda λ﻿ be a scalar. Then the following are equivalent
      1. λ\lambdaλ﻿ is an eigenvalue of AAA﻿.
      1. λ\lambdaλ﻿ is a solution of the equation det(λIn−A)=0\textrm{det}(\lambda I_n-A) = 0det(λIn​−A)=0﻿.
      1. The linear system (λIn−A)x=0(\lambda I_n-A)x = 0(λIn​−A)x=0﻿ has nontrivial solutions.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a triangular matrix. Then the eigenvalue of AAA﻿ are the entries on the main diagonal of AAA﻿.
    • Let λ\lambdaλ﻿ be an eigenvalue of a matrix AAA﻿ and xxx﻿ be a corresponding eigenvector. If r∈Nr\in \Nr∈N﻿, then λr\lambda^rλr﻿ is an eigenvalue of ArA^rAr﻿ and xxx﻿ is a corresponding eigenvector.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. The reduced row echelon form of AAA﻿ is InI_nIn​﻿.
      1. AAA﻿ is expressible as a product of elementary matrices.
      1. AAA﻿ is invertible.
      1. Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ has only the trivial solution.
      1. Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ has consistent for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ has exactly one solution for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. The column vectors of AA A﻿ are linearly independent.
      1. The row vectors of AAA﻿ are linearly independet.
      1. det(A)≠0.\textrm{det}(A)\neq0.det(A)​=0.﻿
      1. λ=0\lambda=0 λ=0﻿ is not an eigenvalue of AAA﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times n n×n﻿ matrix. Then the characteristic polynomial of AAA﻿ can be expressed as

      where λ1,λ2,⋯ ,λk\lambda_1,\lambda_2,\cdots,\lambda_kλ1​,λ2​,⋯,λk​﻿ are the distinct eigenvalues of AAA﻿ and m1+m2+⋯+mk=nm_1+ m_2+\cdots+m_k = nm1​+m2​+⋯+mk​=n﻿.

    • Let AAA﻿ be a 2×22\times 22×2﻿ matrix with real entries. Then the characteristic equatoin of AAA﻿ is

      and the following statements hold:

      1. AAA﻿ has two distinct real eigenvalues if tr(A)2−4det(A)>0\textrm{tr}(A)^2-4\textrm{det}(A) > 0tr(A)2−4det(A)>0﻿
      1. AAA﻿ has one repeated real eigenvalue if tr(A)2−4det(A)=0\textrm{tr}(A)^2-4\textrm{det}(A) = 0tr(A)2−4det(A)=0﻿
      1. AA A﻿ has two conjugate imaginary eigenvalues if tr(A)2−4det(A)<0\textrm{tr}(A)^2-4\textrm{det}(A) < 0tr(A)2−4det(A)<0﻿
    • A 2×22\times 22×2﻿ symmetric matrix AAA﻿ with real entries has real eigenvalues. Moreover, if AAA﻿ is of the form

      then it has one repeated eigenvalue, namely λ=a\lambda=aλ=a﻿; otherwise it has two distinct eigenvalues.

    • Let AAA﻿ be a 2×22\times 22×2﻿ symmetric matrix with real entries. Then the following hold.
      1. If AAA﻿ has one repeated eigenvalue, then the eigenspace corresponding to that eigenvalue is R2\R^2R2﻿.
      1. If AAA﻿ has two distinct eigenvalues, then the eigenspaces corresponding to those eigenvalues are perpendicular lines through the origin of R2\R^2R2﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times n n×n﻿ matrix with eigenvalues λ1,λ2,⋯ ,λn\lambda _1, \lambda_2, \cdots ,\lambda_nλ1​,λ2​,⋯,λn​﻿ (repeated according to multiplicity). Then the following hold.
      1. det(A)=λ1λ2⋯λn\textrm{det}(A)=\lambda_1\lambda_2\cdots\lambda_ndet(A)=λ1​λ2​⋯λn​﻿.
      1. tr(A)=λ1+λ2+⋯λn\textrm{tr}(A) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots \lambda_ntr(A)=λ1​+λ2​+⋯λn​﻿.

6.1 Matrices as Transformations

  • Definitions
    • A function whose inputs and outputs are vectors is called a transformation. If TT T﻿ is a transformation from Rn\R^n Rn﻿ to Rm\R^mRm﻿, then the set RmR^mRm﻿ is called the codomain of TTT﻿. A transformation is called an operator on Rn\R^n Rn﻿ if it maps Rn\R^nRn﻿ into Rn\R^nRn﻿.
    • Let A=(aij)A = (a_{ij}) A=(aij​)﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. Define

      for each vector x=(x1,x2,⋯ ,xn)x=(x_1,x_2,\cdots,x_n)x=(x1​,x2​,⋯,xn​)﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿ where ai=(ai1,ai2,⋯ ,ain)a_i = (a_{i1},a_{i2},\cdots,a_{in})ai​=(ai1​,ai2​,⋯,ain​)﻿ is the ii i﻿th row vector of AAA﻿ for i=1,2,⋯ ,mi=1,2,\cdots, mi=1,2,⋯,m﻿. The trasformation TAT_ATA​﻿ from Rn\R^nRn﻿ to Rm\R^mRm﻿ is called the transformation represented by AAA﻿ or the multiplication by AAA﻿.

    • A function T:Rn→RmT: \R^n\rightarrow\R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ is called a linear transformation from Rn\R^nRn﻿ to Rm\R^mRm﻿ if the following two properties hold for all vectors u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿ and all scalar ccc﻿:
      1. (Homogeneity property) T(cu)=cT(u)T(c\textbf{u}) =c T(\textbf{u})T(cu)=cT(u)﻿
      1. (Additivity property) T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v)T(\textbf{u} + \textbf{v}) = T(\textbf{u}) + T(\textbf{v})T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v)﻿

      In the case where m=nm=nm=n﻿, the linear transformation TTT﻿ is called a linear operator on RnR^nRn﻿.

    • A rotation about the origin in R2\R^2R2﻿ through an angle is defined to be a transformation that rotates each vector xxx﻿ in R2\R^2R2﻿ about the origin through an angle θ\thetaθ﻿.
    • A reflection about a line through the origin in R2\R^2R2﻿ is defined to be a transformation that maps each vector xxx﻿ in R2\R^2R2﻿ into its mirror image about a line LLL﻿ through the origin.
    • An orthogonal projection onto a line through the origin in R2\R^2R2﻿ is defined to be a transformation that projects each vector xxx﻿ in R2\R^2R2﻿ onto a line LLL﻿ through the origin by dropping a perpendicular to LLL﻿.
  • Theorems
    • Let T:Rn→RmT: \R^n\rightarrow\R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a linear transformation. Let u\textbf{u}u﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ be vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿. Then the following hold.
      1. T(0)=0T(0) = 0T(0)=0﻿
      1. T(−u)=−T(u)T(-\textbf{u}) = -T(\textbf{u})T(−u)=−T(u)﻿
      1. T(u−v)=T(u)−T(v)T(\textbf{u}-\textbf{v}) = T(\textbf{u}) - T(\textbf{v}) T(u−v)=T(u)−T(v)﻿
    • Let T:Rn→RmT: \R^n\rightarrow\R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a linear transformation. Let e1,e2,⋯ ,en\textbf{e}_1,\textbf{e}_2,\cdots , \textbf{e}_ne1​,e2​,⋯,en​﻿ be the standard unit vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿ and xxx﻿ be a vector in Rn\R^nRn﻿. Then T(x)T(x)T(x)﻿ can be expressed as

      where AAA﻿ is the m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix given by

6.2 Geometry of Linear Operators

  • Definitions
    • A linear operator T:Rn→RnT :\R^n \rightarrow \R^nT:Rn→Rn﻿ is called an orthogonal operator or a linear isometry if

      for all vector xxx﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.

    • A square matrix AAA﻿ is said to be orthogonal if

      or equivalently, ATA=IA^\mathsf{T}A = I ATA=I﻿

    • Let kk k﻿ be a nonnegative scalar. The linear operator TTT﻿ on R2\R^2R2﻿ defined by

      for each vector (x,y)(x,y)(x,y)﻿ in R2\R^2R2﻿ is called the scaling operator with factor kkk﻿. If 0≤k<10\leq k < 10≤k<1﻿, then TTT﻿ is called a contraction. If k>1k>1k>1﻿, then TT T﻿ is called a dilation. The standard matrix for TTT﻿ is

    • Let kk k﻿ be a nonnegative scalar. For each vector (x,y)(x,y)(x,y)﻿ in R2\R^2R2﻿, let

      If k>1k>1k>1﻿, then TT T﻿ is called a horizontal expansion . If 0≤k<10\leq k < 10≤k<1﻿, then TTT﻿ is called a horizontal contraction. The standard matrix for TTT﻿ is

    • Let kk k﻿ be a nonnegative scalar. For each vector (x,y)(x,y)(x,y)﻿ in R2\R^2R2﻿, let

      If k>1k>1k>1﻿, then TT T﻿ is called a vertical expansion . If 0≤k<10\leq k < 10≤k<1﻿, then TTT﻿ is called a vertical contraction. The standard matrix for TTT﻿ is

    • Let kk k﻿ be a nonzero scalar. The linear operator TTT﻿ on R2\R^2R2﻿ defined by

      for each vector (x,y)(x,y)(x,y)﻿ in R2\R^2R2﻿ is called the vertical shear with factor kkk﻿. The standard matrix for TTT﻿ is

  • Theorems
    • Let T:Rn→RmT: \R^n\rightarrow\R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a linear operator. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. ∥T(x)∥=∥x∥\rVert T(x)\rVert = \rVert x\rVert∥T(x)∥=∥x∥﻿ for all vectors xxx﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿, i.e., TT T﻿ is orthogonal or length preserving.
      1. T(x)⋅T(y)=x⋅yT(x)\cdot T(y)=x\cdot yT(x)⋅T(y)=x⋅y﻿ for all vectors xxx﻿ and yyy﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿, i.e., TTT﻿ is dot product preserving.
    • The following statements hold.
      1. The transpose of an orthogonal matrix is orthogonal.
      1. The inverse of an orthogonal matrix is orthogonal.
      1. A product of orthogonal matrices is orthogonal.
      1. If AAA﻿ is orthogonal, then det(A)=1\textrm{det}(A)=1det(A)=1﻿ or det(A)=−1\textrm{det}(A)=-1det(A)=−1﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. ATA=IA^\mathsf{T} A =IATA=I﻿.
      1. ∥Ax∥=∥x∥\rVert Ax \rVert = \rVert x \rVert∥Ax∥=∥x∥﻿ for all vectors xxx﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. Ax⋅Ay=x⋅yAx\cdot Ay = x\cdot yAx⋅Ay=x⋅y﻿ for all vectors xx x﻿ and yyy﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. AAA﻿ is orthogonal
      1. ∥Ax∥=∥x∥\rVert Ax\rVert =\rVert x\rVert ∥Ax∥=∥x∥﻿ for all vectors xxx﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. Ax⋅Ay=x⋅yAx\cdot Ay = x\cdot yAx⋅Ay=x⋅y﻿ for all vectors xx x﻿ and yyy﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. The column vectors of AAA﻿ are orthogonal.
      1. The row vectors of AAA﻿ are othogonal.
    • A linear operator T:Rn→RnT: \R^n \rightarrow \R^nT:Rn→Rn﻿ is orthogonal if and only if the standard matrix for TT T﻿ is orthogonal.
    • Let T:R2→R2T: \R^2\rightarrow \R^2T:R2→R2﻿ be an orthogonal linear operator. Then the standard matrix for TT T﻿ is expressible in the form

      or

    • Let u=(a,b,c)\textbf{u}=(a,b,c)u=(a,b,c)﻿ be a unit vector in R3\R^3R3﻿. Then the standard matrix Ru,θR_{\textbf{u},\theta}Ru,θ​﻿ for the rotation through an angle θ\thetaθ﻿ about an axis through the origin with orientation u\textbf{u}u﻿ is

      where ω=1−cos⁡θ\omega=1-\cos \thetaω=1−cosθ﻿

6.3 Kernel and Range

  • Definitions
    • Let T:Rn→RmT: \R^n \rightarrow \R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a linear transformation. The set of vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿ that maps into 000﻿ is called the kernel of TTT﻿ and is denoted by ker(T)\textrm{ker}(T)ker(T)﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. The solution space of the linear system Ax=0Ax=0 Ax=0﻿ or equivalently, the kernel of the transformation TAT_ATA​﻿, is called the null space of the matrix AAA﻿ and is denoted by null(A)\textrm{null}(A)null(A)﻿.
    • Let T:Rn→RmT:\R^n\rightarrow \R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a linear transformation. The range of TTT﻿, denoted by ran(T)\textrm{ran}(T)ran(T)﻿, is denoted to be the set of all vectors in Rm\R^mRm﻿ that are images of at least one vector in Rn\R^nRn﻿
    • A linear transformation T:Rn→RmT:\R^n\rightarrow \R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ is said to be onto if the range of TTT﻿ is the codomain Rm\R^mRm﻿, that is, every vector in Rm\R^mRm﻿ is the image of at least one vector in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
    • A linear transformation T:Rn→RmT:\R^n\rightarrow\R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ is said to be one-to-one if TTT﻿ maps distinct vectors in Rn\R^nRn﻿ into distinct vectors in Rm\R^mRm﻿.
  • Theorems
    • Let TT T﻿ be a linear transformation. Then the kernel of TTT﻿ is a subspace of Rn\R^nRn﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times n m×n﻿ matrix. Then the kernel of the transformation represented by AAA﻿ is the solution space of the linear system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿.
    • Let T:Rn→RmT: \R^n \rightarrow \R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a linear transformation. Then TTT﻿ maps subspace of RnR^nRn﻿ into subspace of RmR^mRm﻿.
    • Let T:Rn→RmT:\R^n\rightarrow \R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a linear transformation. Then the range of TT T﻿ is a subspace of Rm\R^mRm﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. Then the range of the transformation represented by AAA﻿ is the subspace of Rm\R^mRm﻿ that is spanned by the column vectors of AAA﻿.
    • Let T:Rn→RmT:\R^n \rightarrow \R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a linear transformation. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. TTT﻿ is one-to-one
      1. ker(T)={0}\textrm{ker}(T) = \{0\}ker(T)={0}﻿
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. Then the transformation TA:Rn→RmT_A:\R^n\rightarrow \R^mTA​:Rn→Rm﻿ represented by AA A﻿ is one-to-one if and only if the linear system Ax=0Ax=0 Ax=0﻿ has only the trivial solution.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix. Then the transformation TA:Rn→RmT_A:\R^n\rightarrow \R^mTA​:Rn→Rm﻿ represented by AA A﻿ is onto if and only if the linear system Ax=bAx =b Ax=b﻿ is consistent for every vector bbb﻿ in Rm\R^mRm﻿.
    • Let T:Rn→RnT:\R^n\rightarrow\R^nT:Rn→Rn﻿ be a linear operator on Rn\R^nRn﻿. Then TTT﻿ is one-to-one if and only if it is onto.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times nn×n﻿ matrix and TA:Rn→RnT_A : \R^n\rightarrow \R^n TA​:Rn→Rn﻿ be the linear transformation represented by A AA﻿. Then the following are equivalent.
      1. The reduced row echelon form of AAA﻿ is InI_nIn​﻿.
      1. AAA﻿ is expressible as a product of elementary matrices.
      1. AAA﻿ is invertible.
      1. Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0﻿ has only the trivial solution.
      1. Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ has consistent for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. Ax=bAx=bAx=b﻿ has exactly one solution for every vector bbb﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.
      1. The column vectors of AA A﻿ are linearly independent.
      1. The row vectors of AAA﻿ are linearly independet.
      1. det(A)≠0.\textrm{det}(A)\neq0.det(A)​=0.﻿
      1. λ=0\lambda=0 λ=0﻿ is not an eigenvalue of AAA﻿.
      1. TAT_ATA​﻿ is one-to-one.
      1. TAT_ATA​﻿ is onto.

6.4 Composition and Invertibility of Linear Transformations

  • Definitions
    • Let T1:Rn→RkT_1:\R^n\rightarrow \R^kT1​:Rn→Rk﻿ and T2:Rk→RmT_2: \R^k\rightarrow\R^mT2​:Rk→Rm﻿ be linear transformations in which the codomain of T1T_1T1​﻿ is the same as the domain of T2T_2T2​﻿. The composition of T2T_2T2​﻿ with T1T_1T1​﻿, denoted by T2∘T1T_2\circ T_1T2​∘T1​﻿, is defined to be the transformation from Rn\R^nRn﻿ to Rm\R^mRm﻿ such that

      for each vector xxx﻿ in Rn\R^nRn﻿.

    • Let

      where λ1,λ2,⋯ ,λn\lambda_1,\lambda_2,\cdots,\lambda_nλ1​,λ2​,⋯,λn​﻿ are nonnegative scalars. Let e1,e2,⋯ ,en\textbf{e}_1,\textbf{e}_2,\cdots, \textbf{e}_n e1​,e2​,⋯,en​﻿ be the standard unit vectors in Rn\R^n Rn﻿. Multiplication by DDD﻿ causes an expansion in the direction of ei\textbf{e}_iei​﻿ if λi>1\lambda_i >1 λi​>1﻿ and a compression in the direction of ei\textbf{e}_i ei​﻿ if 0≤λi<10\leq \lambda _i < 1 0≤λi​<1﻿. Multiplication by DDD﻿ has no effect in the direction of ei\textbf{e} _i ei​﻿ if λi=1\lambda_i =1λi​=1﻿. This matrix is called scaling matrix.

    • Let T:Rn→RmT: \R^n \rightarrow \R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a one-to-one linear transformation. Each vector w\textbf{w}w﻿ in the range of TTT﻿ is the image of a unique vector xxx﻿ in the domain of TTT﻿ which is called the preimage of w\textbf{w}w﻿. A new function from the range of TTT﻿ to Rn\R^n Rn﻿ that maps w\textbf{w}w﻿ into xxx﻿ is called the inverse of TTT﻿ and is denoted by T−1T^{-1}T−1﻿. A one-to-one linear operator is called an invertible linear operator.
    • Let AAA﻿ be an n×nn\times n n×n﻿ invertible matrix and T:Rn→RnT:\R^n \rightarrow \R^nT:Rn→Rn﻿ be the transformation represented by AAA﻿. If v\textbf{v}v﻿ is a vector in Rn\R^nRn﻿ and Av=λvA\textbf{v} = \lambda\textbf{v}Av=λv﻿ for some scalar λ\lambdaλ﻿, then T(v)=λvT(\textbf{v}) = \lambda\textbf{v}T(v)=λv﻿, that is, TTT﻿ maps the vector v\textbf{v}v﻿ onto a vector that is parallel to v\textbf{v}v﻿. If v≠0\textbf{v} \neq0v​=0﻿, then the scalar λ\lambdaλ﻿ is called an eigenvalue of TTT﻿ and v\textbf{v}v﻿ is called an eigenvector of TTT﻿ corresponding to λ\lambdaλ﻿.v
  • Theorems
    • Let T1:Rn→RkT_1: \R^n\rightarrow \R^kT1​:Rn→Rk﻿ and T2:Rk→RmT_2: \R^k\rightarrow\R^mT2​:Rk→Rm﻿ be linear transformations. Then T2∘T1:Rn→RmT_2\circ T_1:\R^n\rightarrow\R^mT2​∘T1​:Rn→Rm﻿ is a linear transformation.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a k×nk\times n k×n﻿ matrix and BBB﻿ be an m×km\times km×k﻿ matrix. Then the m×nm\times nm×n﻿ matrix BABABA﻿ is the standard matrix for the composition of the linear transformation represented by BBB﻿ with the linear transformation represented by AAA﻿.
    • Let T1,T2,⋯ ,TkT_1, T_2,\cdots,T_kT1​,T2​,⋯,Tk​﻿ be a succession of rotations about axees through the origin in R3\R^3R3﻿. Then the kkk﻿ notations can be accomplished by a single rotation about some appropriate axis through the origin in R3\R^3R3﻿.
    • Let AAA﻿ be a 2×22\times 2 2×2﻿ invertible matrix. Then the linear operator represented by AAA﻿ is a composition of shears, compressions and expansions in the direction of the coordinate axes and reflections about the coordinate axes and about the line y=xy=xy=x﻿.
    • Let T:Rn→RmT: \R^n\rightarrow\R^mT:Rn→Rm﻿ be a linear transformation. If TTT﻿ is one-to-one, then T−1T^{-1}T−1﻿ is one-to-one.
    • Let T:Rn→RnT: \R^n\rightarrow\R^nT:Rn→Rn﻿ be a linear operator. If TTT﻿ is one-to-one, then the standard matrix AAA﻿ for TTT﻿ is invertible and its inverse is the standard matrix for T−1T^{-1}T−1﻿, i.e.,
    • Let T:R2→R2T: \R^2\rightarrow \R^2T:R2→R2﻿ be an invertible linear operator. Then the following hold.
      1. The image of a line is a line
      1. The image of a line passes through the origin if and only if the original line passes through the origin
      1. The images of two lines are parallel if and only if the original lines are parallel
      1. The images of three points lie on a line if and only if the original points lie on a line.
      1. The images of the line segment joining two points is the line segment joining the images of those points.
    • Let T:R2→R2T: \R^2\rightarrow \R^2T:R2→R2﻿ be an invertible linear operator. Then TTT﻿ maps the unit square into a nondegenerate parallelogram that has a vertex at the origin and has adjacent sides T(e1)T(\textbf{e}_1) T(e1​)﻿ and T(e2)T(\textbf{e}_2)T(e2​)﻿. The area of the parellelogram is ∣det(A)∣|\textrm{det}(A)|∣det(A)∣﻿ where A=[T(e1)∣T(e2)]A = [T(\textbf{e}_1) \mid T(\textbf{e}_2) ] A=[T(e1​)∣T(e2​)]﻿ is the standard matrix for TTT﻿.
